one_line_takeaway: A Ridge-regression meta-model trained on fused gray-/black-box LLM output signals (log-prob entropy/margin, verbalized confidence, and novel aleatoric-vs-epistemic abstention flags) predicts LLM moderation errors well enough to cut cost-aware human-review escalation cost by up to 71% on text moderation, though gains are inconsistent for open-source models under multimodal distribution shift.
key_finding: On the text-only OpenAI Moderation Dataset, the meta-model beat the strongest per-row baseline for every reported LLM, e.g. gpt-4o-mini: F1 81.27%->94.14%, AUC-ROC 83.55%->93.46%, Macro-F1 64.96%->82.31%; and cost-side, gpt-4o-mini expected cost fell from $132 (best baseline: Top-2 Margin) to $38 (a 71% reduction) with escalations dropping from 331 to 148 (escalation ratio 37%->16%). Similar cost reductions were reported for gemini-2.5-flash-lite ($122->$77, 36% reduction) and gemini-2.0-flash-lite ($74->$41, 41% reduction). On the harder Multimodal Moderation Dataset results were model-dependent: gains held for gpt-4o-mini, gpt-4o, and especially gemini-2.0-flash-lite (F1 69.85%->85.47%, cost $80->$45), but QWEN3-14B and LLAMA32-11B showed comparable or worse cost/F1 than the Top-2 Margin baseline, and gpt-4.1-mini and gemini-2.5-flash-lite showed Macro-F1 decreases (72.39%->69.02% and 64.77%->57.59% respectively) despite F1 improving. Ablations showed all three LPP feature families (Post-Hoc Classification Uncertainty, Verbalized Confidence, Uncertainty Attribution Indicators) are complementary -- removing any one increases cost, with Post-Hoc Classification Uncertainty features mattering most, verbalized confidence adding ~5-15% cost when removed, and the binary Uncertainty Attribution Indicators still measurably reducing cost despite their simplicity, especially for gemini-2.5-flash-lite and gemini-2.0-flash-lite.
core_contribution: The paper introduces LLM Performance Predictors (LPPs): a supervised meta-model (Ridge Regression, L2-regularized, sigmoid/isotonic-calibrated) trained on a fused set of gray-box (top-5 token log-probabilities/entropy, top-2 margin, max softmax probability, filtered vs. unfiltered class-token log-probs) and black-box (verbalized confidence scalar + confidence bands) output signals to predict whether a base LLM's content-moderation decision is correct. On top of this the authors add (1) a cost-aware thresholded escalation policy that converts the meta-model's correctness score into a trust-vs-escalate decision by minimizing an explicit expected-cost function C(tau) = c_mis*FP + (c_rev - c_mis)*TN + c_rev*FN, with the threshold tau swept in [0.35,0.70] on a held-out validation split, and (2) novel moderation-specific 'Uncertainty Attribution Indicators' -- a 4-way integer output schema (0=no,1=yes,2=inconclusive_evidence [aleatoric],3=inconclusive_definition [epistemic]) that lets the system route 'Tough Calls' (evidence deficits) to senior reviewers and 'Policy Gaps' (definitional ambiguity) to policy-update/active-learning loops. All three pieces (the fused LPP feature set + meta-model, the cost-aware routing policy, and the attribution indicators) are the paper's own contribution, evaluated end to end on real moderation datasets against post-hoc uncertainty baselines.
threat_model: None in the adversarial-ML sense -- there is no attacker, no adversarial input crafting, and no poisoning/jailbreak scenario considered. The 'risk' modeled is purely operational trust-and-safety risk: an LLM silently misclassifying policy-violating content (hate, violence, self-harm, sexual content, drugs/alcohol/tobacco, death/injury/military conflict, kids content) and that error reaching production undetected (a false positive in their cost formulation, i.e., trusting an incorrect LLM output), traded off against the operational cost of unnecessary human review (false negative, i.e., escalating a correct LLM output). The paper explicitly frames content moderation as 'a cornerstone of online trust and safety' and targets the decision of when to trust vs. escalate an LLM's moderation call to a human reviewer -- a trust-and-safety/reliability problem, not a security-attack problem.
limitations: Self-reported by the authors in Section 5.5: (1) the meta-model requires labeled ground-truth moderation data, incurring annotation cost; (2) the framework is limited to post-response (post-generation) LPPs computed after the LLM answers -- no pre-response/anticipatory predictors; (3) all evaluated base models are transformer-based autoregressive LLMs, so generalization to other architectures (retrieval-augmented, neuro-symbolic) is untested; (4) the cost model uses fixed review/error penalties whereas real costs vary by severity, reviewer expertise, and platform risk tolerance; (5) CoT-derived features were extracted but not used in the reported pipeline because CoT inflated confidence and hurt calibration in this setting; (6) QWEN3-32B and LLAMA32-11B were excluded from the OpenAI Moderation Dataset results (compute constraints and output-parsing failures respectively); (7) safety-specialized models such as Llama Guard were excluded because their taxonomies didn't align with the paper's moderation categories; (8) results on the Multimodal Moderation Dataset are inconsistent across model families (gains for GPT/Gemini variants, flat or negative for QWEN/LLAMA), which the authors attribute to calibration challenges and majority-class bias under distribution shift.

datasets_used: OpenAI Moderation dataset (1,680 English text samples; hate, self-harm, sexual, violence categories with subcategories; multi-label, evaluated at text-label-pair granularity)

Multimodal Moderation Dataset (1,500 short-form videos; 12 languages; four modalities text/thumbnail/transcript/video-frames; categories DIMC=Death/Injury/Military Conflict, DAT=Drugs/Alcohol/Tobacco, Kids)
quantitative_results: gpt-4o-mini (OpenAI Moderation Dataset): F1 81.27%->94.14%, AUC-ROC 83.55%->93.46%, Macro-F1 64.96%->82.31% vs. best baseline
gpt-4o-mini (OpenAI Moderation Dataset) expected cost: $132 (best baseline Top-2 Margin) -> $38 (meta-model), 71% reduction; escalations 331->148 (37%->16% ratio)
gemini-2.5-flash-lite (OpenAI Moderation Dataset) expected cost: $122->$77 (36% reduction); escalations 315->227
gemini-2.0-flash-lite (OpenAI Moderation Dataset) expected cost: $74->$41 (41% reduction); escalations 248->162
gpt-4o-mini (Multimodal Moderation Dataset): F1 85.71%->87.34%, AUC-ROC 83.95%->88.71%, Macro-F1 71.20%->74.07%; cost $37->$22 (41% reduction), escalations 107->80
gemini-2.0-flash-lite (Multimodal Moderation Dataset): F1 69.85%->85.47% (largest F1 gain), Macro-F1 52.67%->61.09%; cost $80->$45
gpt-4.1-mini (Multimodal Moderation Dataset): Macro-F1 decreased 72.39%->69.02% despite F1 gain, indicating a calibration trade-off
Class imbalance: base LLMs correct in roughly 80-90% of moderation cases (10-20% error rate) before downsampling
